claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you have
- Their ages (or dates of birth)
- Their names (so I can provide specific guidance for each child)

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. His name is Alex and he's 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! Alex is 8 years old, which is under the age limit for Child Benefit.

Now I need to ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Do you live in the UK, or are you a UK citizen living abroad in specific circumstances (such as working for a UK employer or a Crown servant)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex:

**Are you Alex's parent, or do you have parental responsibility for him?** (For example, are you his birth parent, adoptive parent, legal guardian, or do you have a legal parental responsibility order?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm Alex's parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to ask about Alex's living arrangements:

**Does Alex live with you?** If yes, does he live with you full-time, or does he split his time between you and someone else (like another parent)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. He lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask:

**Do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would provide towards Alex's upkeep?** 

For context, Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child (and £16.95 per week for additional children). So the question is whether you contribute at least £25.60 per week towards Alex's care and upkeep.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help out with things like clothes, school expenses, and other bits and pieces. I give about £26 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask an important question about who else might be claiming:

**Is Alex's mum (or anyone else Alex lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, his mum isn't claiming Child Benefit for him. Nobody else is claiming it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more important question:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations:**
- In full-time non-advanced education or approved training (this would typically apply to children aged 16-19, so likely not relevant for an 8-year-old)
- In hospital or local authority care for an extended period?
- Living with someone else under a formal care arrangement?

Or is he just living a normal life with his mum - going to school, living at home as usual?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing like that. He's just living a normal life with his mum - going to primary school, the usual things an 8-year-old does.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16, so within the age limit)
✓ You are Alex's parent (you have parental responsibility)
✓ You live in the UK
✓ Alex does not live with you, but lives with his mum
✓ You contribute at least £25.60 per week towards Alex's upkeep
✓ His mum is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex
✓ Alex is in a normal living situation

However, there's an important rule I need to explain: **Generally, Child Benefit is paid to the person the child lives with**, as they typically have the main day-to-day responsibility for the child's care.

Since Alex lives with his mum, she would normally be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit, even if no one is currently claiming it. The fact that you contribute financially is positive, but the key factor is that the child lives with her.

**You would only be eligible to claim if:**
- You contribute at least the amount of Child Benefit towards Alex's upkeep (which you do), AND
- The person Alex lives with agrees not to claim, or there's a reason they cannot claim

**My question is: Have you discussed this with Alex's mum, and is she willing to let you claim the Child Benefit instead of claiming it herself?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, she's not claiming it and hasn't expressed any interest in doing so. We haven't specifically discussed who should claim it, but she's never mentioned wanting to claim it herself. I'm the one contributing financially each week, so I thought I should be able to claim it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. This is a situation where the rules are quite specific, and I want to make sure I give you accurate guidance.

The key rule is that **Child Benefit is normally paid to the person the child lives with** - in this case, Alex's mum. Even though you contribute financially (which is important and commendable), the primary factor for Child Benefit is who the child lives with on a day-to-day basis.

**Here's the situation:**
- Alex's mum has the priority to claim because Alex lives with her
- You can only claim if she either formally agrees not to claim, or if there are circumstances where she cannot or will not claim
- Since she hasn't claimed it yet, this might mean she doesn't want to claim it, but this needs to be clear

**Before I can make a final determination on your eligibility, I need to know:**

**Can you confirm whether Alex's mum has explicitly said she does NOT want to claim Child Benefit and is happy for you to claim it instead?** Or is it just that she hasn't gotten around to claiming it?

This distinction is important because if she simply hasn't claimed yet but would want to if she knew about it, she would have priority over you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Look, we haven't had a formal conversation about it where she's said "I don't want to claim it, you do it instead." It's more that she's never mentioned it or shown any interest in claiming it. I'm the one who's been looking into it and making sure Alex gets what he needs financially. If it matters, I can talk to her about it, but right now she's just not claiming it and I am contributing regularly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I really appreciate your honesty about the situation. Let me give you a clear assessment based on the rules.

**The reality is this:** Under Child Benefit rules, because Alex lives with his mum, **she has the primary entitlement to claim Child Benefit for him**. The law prioritizes the person the child lives with, as they typically have the main day-to-day caring responsibility.

Even though:
- You contribute financially (which is excellent)
- She hasn't claimed it yet
- You're the one taking the initiative

**The rules still give her priority** because Alex lives with her.

**For you to be eligible to claim instead of her, you would need:**
- Her explicit agreement that she won't claim and that you can claim instead
- This is sometimes referred to as showing that you have "priority" because the person the child lives with has chosen not to exercise their entitlement

**My recommendation:** You should have a conversation with Alex's mum about Child Benefit. If she genuinely doesn't want to claim it and is happy for you to do so, then you could proceed with a claim. But without that clear agreement, if you both tried to claim, she would have priority, and your claim could be rejected.

**Would you like me to make a final determination based on your current situation (without her explicit agreement), or would you prefer to speak with her first and come back to me?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I understand what you're saying. Based on what I know now - that she has priority because Alex lives with her, and I don't have her explicit agreement that she won't claim - what's your final determination on my eligibility right now?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Final Determination

**You are NOT currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

**For Alex (8 years old):**
- ✓ He is under 16, so within the age limit
- ✓ You are his parent with parental responsibility
- ✓ You live in the UK
- ✓ You contribute at least £25.60 per week towards his upkeep
- ✗ **However**, Alex lives with his mother, who has priority to claim under Child Benefit rules
- ✗ You do not have her explicit agreement that she will not claim

**The key issue:** Under UK Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority because they have the main day-to-day caring responsibility. While a non-resident parent can claim if they contribute financially AND the resident parent agrees not to claim, you don't currently have that agreement from Alex's mum.

**What you can do:**
If you speak with Alex's mum and she explicitly agrees that she does not want to claim Child Benefit and is happy for you to claim it instead, then you could become eligible. But as things stand right now, without that agreement, your claim would not be successful because she retains the priority right as the person Alex lives with.

I hope this clarifies your situation. Please feel free to ask if you have any other questions!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under the age limit for Child Benefit (under 16). The claimant is Alex's parent and lives in the UK, both of which are positive factors. The claimant also contributes at least £25.60 per week towards Alex's upkeep, meeting the minimum contribution threshold. However, Alex does not live with the claimant - he lives with his mother. Under UK Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority to claim Child Benefit, as they typically have the main day-to-day caring responsibility. While a non-resident parent can claim if they contribute towards the child's upkeep AND the resident parent agrees not to claim, in this case there is no explicit agreement from Alex's mother that she will not claim Child Benefit. Without such an agreement, Alex's mother retains the priority right to claim as the resident parent. Therefore, the claimant is not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is Alex's parent who lives in the UK and contributes financially towards Alex's care (approximately £26 per week). However, Alex lives with his mother, who has not claimed Child Benefit but has not explicitly agreed to forgo her right to claim. Under Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority. Without the mother's explicit agreement not to claim, the claimant cannot successfully claim Child Benefit for Alex at this time. The claimant would need to obtain the mother's clear agreement that she will not claim before being eligible to claim himself.

While a non-resident parent can claim if they contribute towards the child's upkeep AND the resident parent agrees not to claim, in this case there is no explicit agreement from Alex's mother that she will not claim Child Benefit. Without such an agreement, Alex's mother retains the priority right to claim as the resident parent. Therefore, the claimant is not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

However, Alex lives with his mother, who has not claimed Child Benefit but has not explicitly agreed to forgo her right to claim. Under Child Benefit rules, the person the child lives with has priority. Without the mother's explicit agreement not to claim, the claimant cannot successfully claim Child Benefit for Alex at this time. The claimant would need to obtain the mother's clear agreement that she will not claim before being eligible to claim himself.
